question: What was the company's total revenue for each of the last 5 financial years?
What was the net profit for each of the last 5 financial years?
What was the total borrowings (short-term + long-term debt) for each of the last 5 financial years?
What was the operating profit margin (operating profit / revenue × 100) for each of the last 5 financial years?
What was the year-on-year revenue growth rate for each of the last 4 financial years?
What was the free cash flow (operating cash flow minus capital expenditure) for each of the last 5 financial years?
What were the receivable days, inventory days, and payable days for each of the last 3 financial years?
expected_answer: FY24: [Receivable: 24.3, Inventory: 85.4], FY25: [Receivable: 27.2, Inventory: 95.6], FY26: [Receivable: 26.4, Inventory: 102.3]. Payable days not computable from available data — no Trade Payables line item in this dataset.

question: What was the total capex spent over the last 5 financial years combined, and what was the cumulative revenue growth over the same period? Did capex translate into proportional top-line growth?
expected_answer: Total Capex (proxy from Investing CF): 2038.20, Cumulative Revenue Growth: 49.49%. FY22 had net investing inflow of 71.5 (excluded from capex sum). Capex of 2038 Cr drove only 49.5% revenue growth — relatively capital-intensive expansion.

question: In which financial year did the gap between EBITDA and operating cash flow diverge the most, and by how much?
expected_answer: Year: FY25, Divergence: 357.60. EBITDA (PBT + Interest + Depreciation) = 987.20, OCF = 629.60, Gap = 357.60

question: What is the 3-year CAGR of revenue and the 3-year CAGR of total assets? Is the company growing revenue faster or slower than its asset base is expanding?
expected_answer: Revenue 3Y CAGR: 8.39, Asset 3Y CAGR: 10.04. Revenue is growing SLOWER than asset base — indicating declining asset utilization efficiency.

question: What is the debt-to-equity ratio for each of the most recent 3 financial years?
What is the current ratio for each of the most recent 3 financial years?
What is the return on equity (net profit / shareholders' equity × 100) for each of the most recent 3 financial years?
What is the return on capital employed (EBIT / capital employed × 100) for each of the most recent 3 financial years?
What is the interest coverage ratio (EBIT / interest expense) for each of the most recent 3 financial years?

Perform a DuPont decomposition of ROE for the most recent financial year. What are the three components: net profit margin (net profit / revenue), asset turnover (revenue / total assets), and equity multiplier (total assets / shareholders' equity)?
expected_answer: Net Profit Margin: 8.14%, Asset Turnover: 1.13, Equity Multiplier: 1.43, DuPont ROE: 13.18%

question: What is the cash conversion cycle for each of the most recent 2 financial years? Calculate receivable days, inventory days, and payable days separately and show the net cycle.
expected_answer: FY25: Receivable Days: 27.24, Inventory Days: 95.57, Partial CCC (excl Payable Days): 122.81. FY26: Receivable Days: 26.40, Inventory Days: 102.33, Partial CCC (excl Payable Days): 128.73. Payable Days not computable — no Trade Payables line item in available data.

question: What is the operating cash flow to net profit ratio for each of the last 3 financial years? Is the company consistently converting its accounting profits into real cash?
expected_answer: FY24: 1.51, FY25: 1.20, FY26: 2.09. Yes, OCF consistently exceeds net profit across all 3 years, indicating strong cash conversion.

question: What is the EBIT-to-interest-expense ratio and the change in total borrowings for the most recent 2 financial years? Is the company comfortably servicing its debt while reducing or maintaining its debt levels?
expected_answer: FY25: ICR=18.01x, Borrowings change: +113.80 (119.4 to 233.2). FY26: ICR=12.34x, Borrowings change: +17.10 (233.2 to 250.3). ICR is comfortable but declining. Borrowings increased significantly in FY25, then stabilized in FY26.

question: What is the computed ROCE and the computed cost of debt (interest expense / total borrowings) for the most recent 2 financial years? Is the company earning above its cost of borrowing?
expected_answer: FY25: ROCE=19.32%, Cost of Debt=17.71%, Spread=+1.61%. FY26: ROCE=18.45%, Cost of Debt=25.73%, Spread=-7.28%. Company earned above cost of debt in FY25 but NOT in FY26.

question: What are the key revenue drivers and priorities outlined by management for the coming year?
expected_answer: In the FY25 Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani outlined the key revenue drivers/priorities for the coming year: ramping up utilization at the three newly opened plants (Guwahati, Bhubaneswar, Hyderabad); continued focus on 'increasing value-added products' (higher-than-normal EBITDA margin products); value-added launches such as fire-pro pipes (post UL fitting approval) and OPVC; growing exports via the newly opened Dubai overseas office targeting 'Middle East, Saudi Arabia, or select African markets' and Europe; scaling the newly acquired Al Aziz electrofusion fittings/PE-PP pipe business, including gas application pipes as a long-term opportunity; continued R&D spend, including a new silicone range with '100 colour options'; and expanding the adhesives business into rural India via the 'New Bharat concept.' Sandeep Engineer separately emphasized correcting/reviving the UK and US adhesive businesses over 'the next two to three quarters.'

question: What factors did management attribute the company's performance to in the most recent quarter?
expected_answer: For Q3 FY25 (the most recent standalone quarterly earnings call in the provided documents), Sandeep Engineer attributed performance to: overall demand challenges 'due to overall infrastructure spending by the government and liquidity challenges in the market'; despite this, consolidated top line grew a 'marginal... 2%' with EBITDA growth of '9.3%' YoY; pipe vertical 'maintained our volume and grow our EBITDA by 10.9% in spite of huge volatility in polymer prices'; the anti-dumping duty on PVC 'got delayed, which also affected the industry volume growth'; Adhesives India delivered 14.5% growth with 16.4% EBITDA; Paint grew 7.5% with 4% EBITDA; overseas adhesive (UK/US) de-grew 5% but held positive EBITDA of 0.7-1%.

question: What near-term risks or challenges did management highlight during the calls?
expected_answer: Management highlighted several near-term risks/challenges across the calls: (1) uncertainty on timing/quantum of the PVC anti-dumping duty implementation - Hiranand Savlani (Q3 call): 'we don't know when it is going to be announced by the government'; (2) uncertainty on BIS standard implementation timing - referenced as pending in Q3/Q2 calls; (3) subpar/uncertain channel inventory levels - Hiranand Savlani (Q3 call): 'it is subpar. Because so much of volatility there, nobody has a confidence'; (4) polymer/PVC price volatility - Sandeep Engineer (Analyst Meet): PVC price fell 18% in FY25; (5) government infra spending slowdown and liquidity challenges facing contractors - discussed by Hiranand Savlani in the Analyst Meet; (6) UK adhesive business underperformance and manpower/executive performance issues - Sandeep Engineer (Q2 call); (7) potential US tariff war impact - though Hiranand Savlani (Q3 call) stated 'we are not exporting anything to the US market... I don't think this tariff is going to affect anyway to our company.'

question: What is management's volume and margin guidance for the core business divisions?
expected_answer: Hiranand Savlani (Q3 FY25 call, in response to Sneha Talreja/Shravan Shah): 'our normal range will be between 10% to 15% in the volume... next year also we are expecting that we should be definitely going to deliver between 10% to 15% minimum'; pipe margin guidance range reaffirmed at 16%-18% ('I think my range will remain same'); adhesive India margin guidance '14 to 16 we have given the Indian operation'; UK adhesive margin guidance from Sandeep Engineer: 'UK margin will bring back to that 5% to 10%, which has been a historical margin.' In the Q2 FY25 call, Hiranand Savlani guided: 'We are expecting this year to end between 10% to 15% volume growth' and consolidated EBITDA guidance of '15% to 16%.' No specific capacity utilization percentage guidance for next year was given.

question: What was the impact of raw material price volatility, and what inventory loss/gain was reported?
expected_answer: Sandeep Engineer (Q2 FY25 call): 'PVC prices went down by 13.5% during a single quarter... from beginning of November the prices have started an uptrend... due to this antidumping duty in place, the PVC prices went up by INR1 on 1st of November and again by INR2 on 7th of November.' Hiranand Savlani (Q3 FY25 call, on the same anti-dumping duty context): 'Already INR3 price rise is taken place. We are expecting between INR6 to INR8 price rise should happen over a period of time.' Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet, FY25 annual): 'last year alone, the polymer price was down by 18%. Despite a 18% fall... Astral is the only company in the country that has improved its gross profit margin. We have improved the GP by 1%.' Inventory loss/gain impact was also quantified: Hiranand Savlani (Q2 call): 'a slight loss of inventory also in this quarter... between INR10 crores to INR15 crores kind of loss.'

question: What are the capital expenditure (capex) plans and guidance for the current and next fiscal year?
expected_answer: Hiranand Savlani (Q3 FY25 call, in response to Rahul Agarwal): 'I think we should be closing around Rs. 450 crore or so for the full year' (FY25) and for the following year, 'Next year should be somewhere around 250 or so' (FY26), explained by Sandeep Engineer as being lower because 'Kanpur is almost completed' and no major new facility additions are planned, with spending mainly on machinery rather than land/buildings. In the Q2 FY25 call, capex for H1 FY25 was stated as INR282 crores with full-year capex expected to be 'the same line only... now balance will be more than INR100 crores' with Kanpur cited as a driver. In the Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani separately noted 'In the last two years alone, we have pumped in close to ₹1000 crore into this expansion activity, particularly capex,' with FY26 capex guided at '250 to 300 crore max because Kanpur is still yet to be completed.'

question: What capacity additions or plant updates did management detail?
expected_answer: Specific figures given in the Q3 FY25 call (Hiranand Savlani, in response to Keshav Lahoti): 'This year we have already added 47,000 metric tons in 9 month basis' of pipe capacity; Kanpur plant total capacity will be '60,000' metric tons 'in two phases. First will be 30 and then the second will be another 30' with '30 will be coming to FY26 and 30 will be coming to FY27'; Hyderabad plant total planned capacity was '70,000,' of which 'we already completed I think 25,000 metric tonne.' Separately, in the Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani stated capacity has grown at a '10.3% CAGR, while sales are happening at a 13.5% CAGR,' with utilization 'in the 55 to 65 range.' In the Q3 call it was also stated overall capacity increased 'by 36,000 metric tons from 334k to 370k metric tons' during the year.

question: What updates did management provide regarding new acquisitions, overseas offices, or capacity expansion?
expected_answer: Management discussed the acquisition of Al Aziz (Sandeep Engineer, Analyst Meet): rationale was to enter the PE/PP pipe and fittings product line, including German-approved gas fittings and water-line fittings; 'Now we are getting four machines—three are coming, and one more will come later—for making PE and PP pipes in our plants.' No specific investment amount or timeline in months/years was disclosed for this acquisition. The company also opened its 'first overseas office in Dubai' (Hiranand Savlani, Analyst Meet) targeting Middle East/Gulf/Africa exports - no investment figure given. On new geography for Paint, Sandeep Engineer/Hiranand Savlani (Q2 FY25 call) discussed phased launches: Astral Paint launched in Gujarat, then Rajasthan/Jaipur, with plans to enter 'Maharashtra and MP in next 5 to 6 months' - qualitative timeline given but no specific investment figure. UK adhesive technology/backward integration deal was also mentioned (Hiranand Savlani, Q3 call): 'we have just signed the agreement. So in the Q1, that will be available to us' - no investment amount disclosed.

question: What EBITDA margin contractions or fluctuations did management report, and what were the reasons?
expected_answer: Quantified margin impacts disclosed in the Q2 FY25 call (Hiranand Savlani): consolidated EBITDA fell from 17% (last year) to '15.97%, so precisely 16%,' described as 'a 1% drop on the EBITDA'; consolidated EBITDA in absolute terms fell from 'INR233 crores versus this year, INR219 crores, so almost 6% negative EBITDA growth'; UK adhesive EBITDA swung from '9.7% EBITDA' last year to '2% EBITDA' this quarter, and in a later reference from 'INR9.1 crores' to 'a negative EBITDA of minus INR2 crores'; polymer/PVC price fell 'by almost 13.5%' during the quarter; inventory loss was quantified at 'between INR10 crores to INR15 crores.' In the Q3 FY25 call, pipe EBITDA was maintained/grown, with Sandeep Engineer citing 10.9% EBITDA growth for the pipe vertical despite polymer price volatility, and Analyst Meet figures show GP margin improved 'by 1%' despite an 18% polymer price fall for FY25.

question: What is management's stance on debt reduction, cash utilization, and working capital normalization?
expected_answer: No specific debt reduction target was disclosed in any of the three transcripts; Astral is described as net cash positive rather than carrying debt to reduce. Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet): 'Right now, we are sitting on a net cash of ₹464 crore.' On cash utilization/dividend, in the Q2 FY25 call, Pinaki Banerjee asked how the company plans to utilize its cash of 'around Rs. 290 crores'; Hiranand Savlani responded: 'I think by year end we will be able to know what is our next year budget. Based on that we will work out our cash utilization plan and if we don't have any such kind of utilization plan then we will give it to our shareholders' - no specific dividend payout ratio or percentage was committed. On working capital, Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet) noted creditor/debtor/inventory day movements tied to a one-off rupee-appreciation-driven early creditor payoff in March, but gave no specific working-capital-day normalization target or timeline in days.

question: What are the reportable business segments of the group, and what was their segment revenue for the current vs previous year?
expected_answer: Astral Limited (Group) has two reportable business segments — 'Plumbing' and 'Paints and Adhesives'. Segment revenue for FY25 (year ended March 31, 2025) vs FY24: Plumbing — ₹41,963 Million (FY24: ₹41,420 Million); Paints and Adhesives — ₹16,361 Million (FY24: ₹14,994 Million); Total segment revenue — ₹58,324 Million (FY24: ₹56,414 Million). Note: this segment disclosure is presented only in the Consolidated Financial Statements; the Standalone Financial Statements (Note 41) state that no segment disclosure is given standalone since it is presented in the consolidated statements (Ind AS 108).

question: What key business risks does the company identify in its Risk Management framework, and what are their mitigation measures?
expected_answer: Not disclosed as a single itemized list in that exact section beyond the categories shown in the 'Risk Management' framework: Market Competition; Technological Relevance; Environmental and Social Responsibility; Talent Acquisition and Retention; Supply Chain Continuity; Product Quality and Safety; Intellectual Property Protection; Compliance and Regulatory Adherence. For each, a corresponding mitigation measure is listed (e.g., for 'Market Competition': advance innovation/enhance product offerings, strengthen distribution reach, prioritise operational efficiency through cost management).

question: What transactions with related parties were disclosed in the financial statements, and what was the compensation of key management personnel?
expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 37 (Related Party Disclosures) discloses transactions with Subsidiaries, Joint Venture, Enterprises over which KMP have significant influence, KMP, and Relatives of KMP for FY25 vs FY24 (₹ in Million), including: CSR Expenditure – Astral Foundation 126 (108); Interest Income – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 43 (19); Investment in Subsidiaries – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (2,773); Redemption of debentures – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (1,940); Purchase of Goods/Services – Kairamya Journeys LLP 141 (130); Reimbursement of expenses claimed – Astral Coatings Private Limited 60 (49); Remuneration – Sandeep Engineer 152 (139), Hiranand Savlani 59 (42), Others 57 (52); Rent Paid – Jagruti Sandeep Engineer 1 (1), Sandeep Engineer HUF 1 (1); Sale of Goods – Astral Coatings Private Limited 19 (19); Sitting Fees to Independent Directors (0–1 each); Loans Given – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 477 (91); Loans Repaid – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (8); year-end balances: Loans Given outstanding – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 876 (377); Interest accrued on loan – 20 (5); Payables – Sandeep Engineer 47 (45), Others 5 (4); Advance for purchase of non-current investment – Astral Pipes Limited 109 (109). Compensation of Key Management Personnel (short-term benefits): ₹251 Million (FY24: ₹216 Million).

question: What contingent liabilities and commitments were reported by the company at the close of the financial year?
expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 33 (Contingent Liabilities and Commitments Not Provided For), as at March 31, 2025 (with March 31, 2024 comparatives): Contingent Liabilities — In respect of Income Tax: ₹16 Million (₹16 Million); In respect of Goods and Service Tax (GST), Customs Duty, Entry Tax, Value Added Tax and Central Sales Tax: ₹74 Million (₹11 Million). [Separately listed as Commitments, not contingent liabilities: Capital Contracts remaining to be executed (Net of Advances): ₹1,845 Million (₹1,917 Million); Letters of Credit for Purchases: ₹334 Million (₹1,164 Million).]

question: What operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions did the company detail in the annual report?
expected_answer: Directors' Report states that during FY25 the Company increased its installed capacity of the plumbing business by 14.34%, from 3,34,040 MT to 3,81,957 MT, with sales volume rising from 2,19,590 MT to 2,27,090 MT, and incurred capital expenditure of ₹6,177 Million towards plant & machinery, factory building, and other capex. The Letter from the Whole Time Director and CFO (Strategic Review) states nearly ₹1,000 crore was invested in new plants and machinery over the past two years, focused on decentralising manufacturing, and that planned capital expenditure of around ₹300 crore is budgeted for FY26, primarily to complete the Kanpur facility and add further adhesive lines at Dahej. Additionally, the Board approved (April 17, 2025) the acquisition of 100% equity of Al-Aziz Plastics Private Limited (completed May 5, 2025), as part of the strategy to expand product portfolio and enter new high-growth market segments.

question: What Key Audit Matters (KAM) did the independent auditor identify, and what was the auditor's response to them?
expected_answer: The Independent Auditor's Report (Standalone) identifies two Key Audit Matters: (1) Impairment assessment of Goodwill (Note 2(x)(iv)) — Goodwill of ₹2,036 Million as at March 31, 2025, tested annually via discounted cash-flow models of Cash Generating Units (CGUs); flagged as a KAM due to judgmental assumptions on projected revenue growth, operating margins, cash flows, and discount rates. Auditor's response: assessed CGU definitions for compliance, evaluated management's future cash flow forecasts, compared forecasts to actual results, focused on sales growth/discount rate assumptions, recalculated estimates, involved valuation specialists, and assessed disclosures. (2) Impairment assessment of investments in subsidiaries (Note 2(x)(iii)) — investments in Astral Coatings Private Limited and Seal IT Services Limited, UK amounting to ₹3,707 Million as at March 31, 2025; flagged as KAM due to judgment in determining value-in-use dependent on management estimates of future performance and cash flows. Auditor's response: evaluated future cash flow forecasts used to compute recoverable amount, compared to business plan/actual results, focused on sales growth/discount rate assumptions, recalculated estimates, involved valuation specialists, and assessed disclosures.

question: What segment profit, segment assets, and segment liabilities were reported for the company's core segments?
expected_answer: Consolidated Financial Statements, Note 37 (Segment Reporting) — Segment Profit for year ended March 31, 2025 (FY24 in brackets): Plumbing ₹6,126 Million (₹6,054 Million); Paints and Adhesives ₹1,150 Million (₹1,396 Million); Total ₹7,276 Million (₹7,450 Million). Segment Assets as at March 31, 2025 (March 31, 2024): Plumbing ₹28,677 Million (₹24,623 Million); Paints and Adhesives ₹15,603 Million (₹14,222 Million); Total Segment Assets ₹44,280 Million (₹38,845 Million). Segment Liabilities as at March 31, 2025 (March 31, 2024): Plumbing ₹7,521 Million (₹7,657 Million); Paints and Adhesives ₹3,432 Million (₹2,606 Million); Total Segment Liabilities ₹10,953 Million (₹10,263 Million). Note: The report does not disclose a separately labelled 'segment-wise capital employed' figure; only segment assets and segment liabilities are given.

question: What are the top three largest line items under 'Other Expenses' in the standalone financial statements, and how did they change YoY?
expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 30 (Other Expenses), the top 3 largest line items for FY25 (year ended March 31, 2025) vs FY24: (1) Advertisement and Sales Promotions expenses — ₹1,921 Million (FY24: ₹2,197 Million), a decrease of ₹276 Million; (2) Freight and Forwarding — ₹1,507 Million (FY24: ₹1,357 Million), an increase of ₹150 Million; (3) Power and Fuel — ₹1,279 Million (FY24: ₹1,268 Million), an increase of ₹11 Million. Total Other Expenses: ₹7,801 Million (FY24: ₹7,661 Million).

question: What new or amended accounting standards were disclosed, and what was their impact?
expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 2(y) 'New and amended standards' discloses: (1) Ind AS 117 'Insurance Contracts' — notified by MCA on August 12, 2024 under the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Amendment Rules, 2024, effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after April 1, 2024. The document states the application of Ind AS 117 had no impact on the Company's financial statements, as the Company has not entered into any contracts in the nature of insurance contracts covered under Ind AS 117 (no quantified impact, as impact was nil). (2) Amendment to Ind AS 116 'Leases' — Lease Liability in a Sale and Leaseback, notified via the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Second Amendment Rules, 2024, effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after April 1, 2024. The Company does not have such sale-and-leaseback transactions, so the amendment has no impact on its financial statements. Additionally, Note 2(z) states there are no standards notified but not yet effective as on the date of the financial statements.

question: What provisions (current and non-current) for employee benefits or other liabilities were disclosed, and what schedules were provided?
expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 16 (Provisions) discloses only two line items with opening/closing balances (no separate created/reversed/utilized movement schedule is provided): Non-current — Provision for Employee Benefits: ₹82 Million as at March 31, 2025 (₹50 Million as at March 31, 2024); Current — Provision for Employee Benefits: ₹18 Million as at March 31, 2025 (₹7 Million as at March 31, 2024). Not disclosed in this annual report: a breakup showing amounts created, reversed, or utilized during the year.
